coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots based on deep eutectic solvents precursors to detect Cr6+ in environmental water
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruoyao
Zheng, Yi
Zhang, Qiuya
Wu, Zelin
Wang, Lei
Zhang, Juan
Ren, Hongwei
Duan, Erhong
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium is the main compound in industrial wastewater, which is highly toxic and seriously pollutes the environment and endangers human health. The excellent fluorescence performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is widely used to detect heavy metal ions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with excellent fluorescence performance is widely used to detect heavy metal ions. However, the quantitatively structure of CQDs is different to control in the formation due to the uncertain-complex of biomass-based materials. In this work, nitrogen-rich CQDs (N-CQDs) based on 4-A-1,2,4-TZ and glycerol deep eutectic solvents (DESs) was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. The response of the N-CQDs to Cr6+ was linear in the range of 5–100 μM and a detection limit of 0.7043 μM. The results show that N-CQDs can achieve the detection of Cr6+ well. Satisfactory recovery of Cr6+ was also obtained by measuring in the wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Chromium
Static quenching effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs based on DESs
Mechanism of interaction between N-CQDs and Cr6+

National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Key project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20130), Key research and development project of Hebei Province (22373704D). The central government guides local funds for science and technology development (236Z1812G). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for material characterizations.

This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Key project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20130), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2022208046), Key research and development project of Hebei Province (22373704D).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a). TEM image of N-CQDs; (b) The size distribution of N-CQDs; (c-e) Mapping of N-CQDs; (f) EDS spectra of N-CQDs.
(a). Full scale XPS spectrum of N-CQDs; (b) C1s XPS spectrum; (c) N1s XPS spectrum; (d) O1s XPS spectrum; (e) Raman spectrum of N-CQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of (a) 4-Amino-4 H-1,2,4-triazole (4-A-1,2,4-TZ) (b) glycerol; (c) DESs; (d) N-CQDs; (e) 1H NMR spectra of the N-CQDs; (f) 13C NMR spectra of the N-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis spectrum of N-CQDs; (b) Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of N-CQDs; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelength from 350 to 410 nm; (d) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs.

(a) FL spectra of the N-CQDs response to Cr6+ with different concentrations; (b) Nonlinear relationship and (c) Linear relationship between the concentration of Cr6+ and the fluorescence quenching rate (F0-F)/F0, F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs at 375 nm in the presence or absence of Cr6+.
3D fluorescence (a) Cr6+; (b) N-CQDs; (c) N-CQDs mixed with Cr6+ solution 1:3; (d) Under the 365 nm uv light, UV camera obscura images of N-CQDs and N-CQDs mixed with 0.001–500 μM Cr6+ solution.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs mixed with K+ and other heavy metal ions for detection of Cr6+. (Insert: Enlarged fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs mixed with other heavy metal ions for detection of Cr6+.).
FT-IR spectrum of (a) N-CQDs+Cr6+; (b) different reaction time of N-CQDs+Cr6+.
The mechanism of N-CQDs for Cr6+ detection.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of Cr6+.
Spiked recoveries for Cr6+ in real water sample.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots based on deep eutectic solvents precursors to detect Cr6+ in environmental water

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium is the main compound in industrial wastewater, which is highly toxic and seriously pollutes the environment and endangers human health. The excellent fluorescence performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is widely used to detect heavy metal ions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with excellent fluorescence performance is widely used to detect heavy metal ions. However, the quantitatively structure of CQDs is different to control in the formation due to the uncertain-complex of biomass-based materials. In this work, nitrogen-rich CQDs (N-CQDs) based on 4-A-1,2,4-TZ and glycerol deep eutectic solvents (DESs) was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. The response of the N-CQDs to Cr6+ was linear in the range of 5–100 μM and a detection limit of 0.7043 μM. The results show that N-CQDs can achieve the detection of Cr6+ well. Satisfactory recovery of Cr6+ was also obtained by measuring in the wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial wastewater is mainly composed of hexavalent compounds [1]. Cr(VI) causes health problems, skin problems and even cancer risk. In recent years, chromium has become a common dopant and catalyst for the production of new materials and renewable energy, thus contributing to the sustainable development of technologies [2]. However, chromium and its compounds cause environment pollution, which mainly comes from leather preparations, industrial pigments, inferior cosmetic raw materials, chromium-plated parts of metal parts, rubber and so on [3]. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to find a method with high selectivity, strong sensitivity, rapid response time, for detection of Cr6+.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted much attention in the past decade [4,5]. Among them, CQDs have the characteristics of low cost, low toxicity and high fluorescence, especially its excellent optical and electrical properties, it has been widely used in photocatalysis, electrochemistry, biological imaging, fluorescence sensing and various other research fields [6–10]. Up to now, various carbon sources have been developed to prepare CQDs, especially biomass-based materials. These raw materials not only have the advantages of multiple varieties and wide distribution, but also have good biodegradability and regeneration [11–15]. For example, broccoli, sweet potato, garlic, enokitake mushroom, However, the constitute of biomass-based materials are naturally complex, unstable and uncertain due to the environmental factors. The structure can not be determined, and the site activity is uneven [16,17]. Biomass materials can not quantitatively control the content of certain compounds to form CQDs and thus their applications are heavily restricted.

Recently, doping CQDs with nitrogen is one of the most effective strategies to improve the luminescence performance of CQDs [18,19]. N-heterocyclic compounds are mostly used because of their excess nitrogen, important biological activity and strong-active coordination ability. Since then, Nitrogen-rich CQDs exhibit excellent photoelectric properties due to the passivation effect induced by the introduction of nitrogen atoms, which to a large extent makes up the defects of conventional CQDs. There is great potential in the detection wastewater. In some studies, N-CQDs with different precursors can be used as a probe for heavy metal detection [20–22]. However, the results are not satisfied due to the inefficiency with uncontrolled structure of CQDs, which are affluent by too many influencing factors of biomass-based materials.

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) are a kind of recently-emerged green solvents [23–26]. The designable properties of DESs would provide sufficient reaction sites by optimizing the species and molar ratio of precursors. At the same time, the functionalized structure would be helpful to explore the formation mechanism of CQDs. In this study, N-CQDs derived from 4-amino-4 H-1,2,4-triazole based DESs was purposefully designed and applied in the detection of Cr6+ in wastewater. The structural characteristics of the N-CQDs was evaluated. The fluorescence properties were discussed and the optimum conditions for fluorescence test were determined.

4-Amino-4 H-1,2,4-triazole (4-A-1,2,4-TZ), glycerol, anhydrous ethanol, potassium dichromate, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Magnesium(Ⅱ) nitrate hexahydrate(Mg(NO3)2·6 H2O), cuprum(Ⅱ) nitrate trihydrate(Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O), chromic(Ⅲ) nitrate nonahydrate(Cr(NO3)3·9 H2O), aluminum(Ⅲ) nitrate nonahydrate(Al(NO3)3·9 H2O), ferric(Ⅲ) itrate nonahydrate(Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O), lead(Ⅱ) nitrate(Pb(NO3)2), were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using an F20 TEM (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan), with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS) and mapping images were collected using an energy dispersive spectrometer (Aztec Xmax 50, Oxford). Xray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi xray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al K Alpha (hv＝1340 eV) in standard lens mode (ThermoFischer, Waltham, USA). Zeta potential measurements were carried out on a malvern nano-ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Nuclear magnetic spectra (1H NMR, 13C NMR) were recorded using a nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, Avance 500 MHz, Germany) using D2O as the solvent. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained via the KBr pellet method using a Bruker Tensor 27 (Germany) spectrophotometer with wavenumbers from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-6100S UV–vis spectrophotometer (Metash instrument Co., Ltd., China) with a 1.0-cm quartz cuvette. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 150 W lamp as the excitation source. Time-resolved luminescence decays and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) were acquired using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs based on DESs
ce:section.ce:para: In the work, DESs based on 4-A-1,2,4-TZ and glycerol were prepared following previously established methods [27]. 4-A-1,2,4-TZ and glycerol were added into a 250 mL flask at molar ratios of 1:3. The mixtures were heated with stirring until the homogeneous liquid phase appeared. The product was dried under vacuum at 90℃ for 1.5 h, and the product was obtained. The N-CQDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method. Briefly, 1 g of DESs and 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol were added to 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 210℃ for 12 h. Subsequently, the product was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rmp for 10 min. Finally, the obtained supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter.

In order to evaluate the influence of temperature on the fluorescence stability, 10 mL N-CQDs solution was added to 10 mL centrifuge tubes and maintained at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60℃ for 1 h respectively. The solution was transferred to the cuvette quickly and analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The effect of storage time was detected after the N-CQDs solution was kept at 25℃ for 0, 2, 3, 4 and 5 days later.

A solution with varying pH (1−14) was prepared using sodium hydroxide and 37% concentrated hydrochloric acid following a previously established protocol [28]. To determine the influence of pH on the fluorescence stability, 4 mL N-CQDs and 1 mL prepared pH solution from 1 to 14 were added to 10 mL centrifuge tubes, respectively. Subsequently, the mixtures were mixed ultrasonically. The fluorescence spectra were detected by sequence at an excitation wavelength of 375 nm.

The selectivity of N-CQDs for heavy metal ions was explored by comparing the effects of N-CQDs with seven metal ions (Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cr6+) were prepared for further use. Subsequently, N-CQDs were mixed with the above solutions in a 1:3 volume ratio and added into centrifuge tubes. Later, all centrifuge tubes were shaken thoroughly for 10 s and transferred to a spectrophotometer quartz cuvette for fluorescence spectra recording after 1 min.

The water sample was obtained from Shanghai Huayi Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. and the insoluble substance was removed by pretreatment with 0.22 μM microporous membranes. In water sample with different concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ) ions in order to further testing. The analysis process was the same as the standard experiment described above. The samples were measured at blank and spiked at three levels of 0.1, 0.15 and 0.25 μM of Cr6+, respectively.

N-CQDs were synthesized via the one-step solvothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image is shown in Fig. 1(a). The obtained N-CQDs are spherical and distributed evenly. The particle size distribution diagram Fig. 1(b) shows that the N-CQDs were 9–38 nm in size with an average diameter of 22.19 nm. The N-CQDs with different precursor mole ratios (1:2, 1:4) are shown in Fig. S1. The N-CQDs was not successfully produced when the preparation mole ratio was 1:2. When the mole ratio was 1:4, the carbides obtained was black globular, but in smaller quantities, and N-CQDs was not successfully synthesized. Therefore, the precursor mole ratio of 1:3 was chosen as the optimized composition for the N-CQDs preparation.

The zeta potential of N-CQDs was tested. In our experimental design, the testing value was －7.72 mV, proving a specific theoretical basis for the reaction between the prepared N-CQDs and positively charged metal ions. Therefore, it was speculated that there may be many ionizable functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. The data diagram is shown in Fig. S2.

To verify that nitrogen atoms were dopped successfully, EDS and mapping were used to analyze the types of elements and the atomic proportion of N-CQDs (Fig. 1(f)). The concentration of each element and atomic percentage are shown in Table S1. Carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms accounted for 36.74%, 45.00% and 18.26%, respectively. The surface scanning results show that the elements were distributed evenly, and nitrogen atoms were doped successfully with high content to the surface of N-CQDs (Fig. 1(c-e)).

Moreover, XPS measurements were carried out to explore the chemical bonding between the elements. Fig. 2(a) shows the wide full scan XPS spectrum with three distinct peaks at 284.9 eV 399.9 eV, and 531.9 eV, which corresponds to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows four peaks with binding energies around 283.7 eV, 284.2 eV, 285.7 eV, and 287.1 eV, which are assigned to CC/CC, CN, HNCO, CO/CN bonds. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2(c) is deconvoluted into four peaks with binding energies at 399.3 eV, 399.9 eV, 400.6 eV, and 401.6 eV attributed to NH, OCN, NH3 and CN respectively. Fig. 2(d) shows O1s spectrum of bonds CO (531.9 eV) and COC/COH (532.4 eV). To sum up, there is an army of hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, such as NH, COC/COH, and CO groups, resulting in the excellent water solubility of the N-CQDs. Results of XPS show the existence of nitrogen groups and confirm the doping of nitrogen atoms in the prepared N-CQDs.

Based on the above analysis of DESs and N-CQDs, it indicated that the precursor ratio of DESs was related to the internal element content of N-CQDs. When the molar ratio of 4-Amino-4 H-1,2,4-triazole and glycerol was 1:3, the equivalent relative molecular mass of DESs was 360. The weight percent of C, N, O and H were 36.67%, 15.56%, 40.00% and 7.77%, respectively. Combined with the EDS spectra of N-CQDs. The results suggested that the internal element content of N-CQDs could be regulated and controlled by optimizing the ratio of DESs.

The Raman spectrum of N-CQDs exhibits two broad peaks at around 1374 cm−1 and 1565 cm−1, which are attributed to the D (sp3 defects in N-CQDs) and G (stretching vibration within sp2 carbon surface) bands, respectively. The calculated intensity ratio ID/IG is 2.65, indicating the amorphous surface of the N-CQDs in Fig. 2(e) [29,30].

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to determine the surface functional groups of N-CQDs. 4-A-1,2,4-TZ and glycerol form hydrogen bonds in the formation of DESs accompanied by the contraction vibration of CN at 1640 cm−1 is weakened ( Fig. 3(a)) and the absorption peak of the OH off-plane bending vibration at 625 cm−1 is increased (Fig. 3(b)), respectively. Besides, the stretching vibration of the OH bond at 3390 cm−1 is prominent(Fig. 3(c)). In addition, other chemical bonds have not changed substantially, indicating that hydrogen bonds only form DESs and no chemical reaction in this process. However, during the high temperature reaction of the preparation of N-CQDs, some functional groups shrink after the carbonization reaction. As shown in Fig. 3(c) (d), the bending vibration of the C-H bond of DESs at 1210 cm−1, the bending vibration of the =C-H bond in the unsaturated hydrocarbon group at 926 cm−1, and the bending vibration of the C-H bond in p-disubstituted benzene at 852 cm−1 all weakened or even disappeared after the formation of N-CQDs.

This indicates that a chemical reaction has occurred during the synthesis of N-CQDs. From the results of the FT-IR, it can be seen that the surface of N-CQDs contains nitrogenous functional groups. Overall, the XPS analyses are in accordance with the FT-IR results.

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of the N-CQDs served as assistant to characterize the types of carbon and hydrogen in the N-CQDs. In Fig. 3(e), it can be observed that peak at 3.455, and 0.982 ppm were attributable to RNH2, R2NH, CH3CH2-O-R, and RCH3 of N-CQDs, respectively. The peak at 4.658 ppm was attributable to R-OH. As shown in Fig. 3(f), the 13C NMR spectra are mainly divided into four peak groups, the peak at 16.1 ppm is attributed to CH3CH2CH3. The peak at 57.3 ppm belongs to alkyl carbon and N,N-dimethylethylamine carbon, which have higher peaks. The peaks are centered on 62.4, and 72.0 ppm represent carbon atomic absorption of the amines, and ethers, respectively, which have lower peaks. In summary, the types of hydrogen and carbon obtained from this method are consistent with the above results.
As shown in Fig. 4(a), obvious absorption peaks are focused on 210–290 nm in the UV-Vis spectrum of N-CQDs. 240 nm is assigned to the π–π* transition of the CC band.

The red and black lines in Fig. 4(b) represent the fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs, respectively. The excitation spectrum extends from 300 to 450 nm with only one peak located at 375 nm. In the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 350 nm to 410 nm (Fig. 4(c)), the maximum emission wavelength of the fluorescence emission spectrum occurs red-shifted with the increasing excitation wavelength. The intensity of emission peak increases firstly, and then decreases with the redshift of emission peak, indicating a clear excitation dependence of N-CQDs. This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior may be ascribed to N-CQDs with the different emission sites and particle sizes[31]. As illustrated in Fig. 4(d), the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs is 6.69 ns.

In order to study the effect of temperature on fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was measured at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 ℃, respectively. Fig. S3(a) illustrates the fluorescence intensity for N-CQDs derived at different temperatures. It is seen that the change of temperature doesn't result in an obvious variation in the fluorescence intensity. Overall, the fluorescence intensity remains stable, ensuring the N-CQDs have good temperature stability within the range of 10–60 ℃.

Exploring the change of fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs with different days of 0, 2, 3,4, and 5 (Fig. S3(b)), the fluorescence intensity does not change much, indicating that the N-CQDs have good time stability.

To explore the effect of pH on N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs under various pH values from 1 to 14 are listed in Fig. S3(c). As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs remains unchanged under pH＝1–13, and a little lower under strongly basic conditions (pH＝14). It can be concluded that the prepared N-CQDs are suitable for most environment conditions. It indicates that N-CQDs have good pH stability and could provide favorable usage without additional pH adjustment for subsequent detection.

Fig. 5(a) shows the emission spectra of the obtained N-CQDs in different concentrations of Cr6+ solution. Upon increasing the concentration of Cr6+, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs shows gradual reduction. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence quenching rate ((F0-F)/F0) of N-CQDs and concentration of Cr6+ are fitted within the concentration range of 0.003–1000 μM. It conforms to the nonlinear relation, defined by the equation is (F0-F)/F0 ＝ 152.448–151.909/(1+(x/155.9886) ^0.5525), with correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9754. Fig. 5(c) shows the response of the N-CQDs toward Cr6+ at various concentrations from 5 to 100 μM. Excellent linearities (F0-F)/F0 ＝ 0.5172x+16.9537 with correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.9988 were obtained, for which LOD of 0.7043 μM was derived (LOD = 3δ/S), δ is the standard deviation of the blank samples (δ = 0.12142). S denotes the slope of this equation ((F0-F)/F0 ＝ 0.5172x+16.9537, S = 0.5172). The value is below emission standard of domestic drinking water of 0.9616 μM and industrial wastewater of 9.616 μM.

No fluorescence center appeared in the 3D fluorescence pattern of Cr6+ solution alone ( Fig. 6(a)). A pronounced fluorescence center appeared in N-CQDs with the maximum intensity of 7640 a.u. (Fig. 6(b)). However, after adding Cr6+ solution, the fluorescence center of N-CQDs in its original position disappeared, as shown in Fig. 6(c).

Fig. 6(d) shows the image of UV camera obscura of N-CQDs mixed with Cr6+ solution at various concentrations from 0.001 to 500 μM. With the increase of Cr6+ concentration, the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs gradually weakened. When the concentration of Cr6+ solution was 500 μM, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was almost completely quenched.

The prepared N-CQDs were compared with the reported literature, and the results were shown in Table 1 [32–35]. In conclusion, the prepared N-CQDs were entirely suitable for the detection of Cr6+ in actual chromium-containing wastewater.

K2Cr2O7 containing Cr6+ was chosen for the experiment, so the effect of K+ was discussed. As shown in Fig. 7 dotted box, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was 6012 a.u. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs mixed with 250 μM KCl solution was 5656 a.u., and the corresponding fluorescence quenching rate was 5.92%. Also, when N-CQDs was mixed with 250 μM K2Cr2O7 solution, the fluorescence intensity was 400 a.u., and the corresponding fluorescence quenching rate was 93.3%. By contrast, K+ had almost no effect on the detection of Cr6+ in N-CQDs.

This study also investigated the effects of Al3+、Cr3+、Cu2+、Fe3+、Mg2+、Pb2+, on detection of Cr6+ by N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs detection Cr6+ with the addition of other heavy metal ions was almost the same from that of N-CQD detection Cr6+. It shows that other heavy metal ions had no impact on the detection system and the N-CQDs had good specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between N-CQDs and Cr6+
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs and Cr6+ was shown in Fig. 8(a), narrow peak at approximately 3390 cm−1 was converted into a wide peak at approximately from 3572 to 3236 cm−1 after the addition of Cr6+ ions. Also, significantly increased in the intensity for the peak at 1640 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum confirmed the possible binding of Cr6+ ions to the CN groups of the N-CQDs [36]. The absorption of O-H and C-O bonds was weakened by N-CQDs/Cr6+ complex, which indicated that the chemical bonds on the surface of N-CQDs changed. Moreover, with the increase of reaction time, the peak at 1047 cm−1 was blue-shifted Fig. 8(b). Based on these, the fluorescence mechanism of N-CQDs for Cr6+ was proposed.

To elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism for the detection of Cr6+ by N-CQDs, the standard Stern-Volmer equation, given as I
0 /I = 1+ K
SV [Q] = 1+ K
q τ0 [Q], was used to analyze PL quenching. Here, I and I0 represent the PL intensity of N-CQDs in the presence and absence of Cr6+, respectively; Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of Cr6+; Kq corresponds to the bimolecular quenching, and τ0 is the average PL lifetime of the N-CQDs. Kq is calculated as 3.8×1012 M−1 s−1, much larger than the obtained maximal collision quenching constant 2.0×1010 M−1 s−1, manifesting that the PL quenching effects were dominant through a static quenching procedure[37,38]. As shown in Fig S4, the red line and black line represent the fluorescence lifetime curves of N-CQDs (6.69 ns) and N-CQDs-Cr6+ (5.20 ns) mixed solution, respectively. After addition of Cr6+, the difference between the two values is small, and the reduced lifetime indicates electron-transfer process, which demonstrated the existence of static quenching mechanism[39]. The possible synthesis pathways of DESs and N-CQDs and the possible mechanism of Cr6+ detection by N-CQDs were deduced in Fig. 9.

It is obvious that after adding Cr6+, there is a positive shift of zeta potential from −7.72 mV to −1.47 mV (Fig S5). According to previous reports, the fact that the zeta potential becomes more positive is possibly because the deprotonated hydroxyl groups coordinate with the Cr6+ cation, thereby leading to the charge neutralization[40].

The method was applied to environmental water sample to verify the feasibility of Cr6+ detection by N-CQDs. Based on the proposed strategy, Cr6+ was not found in the real sample. After adding Cr6+, the recovery rate of Cr6+ was between 97.9％ and 98.8％, which was within the acceptable range in the analysis, indicating that the N-CQDs was reliable and accurate. In addition, the RSDs of Cr6+ was satisfactory, as shown in Table 2. As such, N-CQDs were considered to be a novel nanomaterial which can detect Cr6+ in real samples quickly.

With DESs (4-Amino-4 H-1,2,4-triazole/glycerol) as precursor, the N-CDs was quantitatively-controlled prepared. The characterization revealed that the N-CQDs were spherical, distributed evenly, with an average size of 22.19 nm. Results of XPS、FT-IR and NMR showed that the N-CQDs had high nitrogen content and a large number of nitrogen groups. The UV-Vis spectrum and fluorescence spectrum showed that the N-CQDs UV absorption peak at 240 nm represent the π-π* transition of the N-N band. There was a prominent emission peak at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm and the fluorescence intensity was 6103 a.u. The response of the N-CQDs to Cr6+ was nonlinear in the range of 0.003–1000 μM and was linear in the range of 5–100 μM with a LOD of 0.7043 μM satisfying the domestic drinking water and industrial emission standard. Experiments were performed on detection of Cr6+ under the conditions of the coexistence of various heavy metal ions, and elimination of the influence of potassium ions. And the results in actual wastewater are good. Therefore, the N-CQDs provides a reliable application in the detection of heavy metal ions in wastewater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors have read the manuscript and agreed upon its submission to your journal. There are no conflicts of interest to other institutions or other people.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2022208046). National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Key project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20130), Key research and development project of Hebei Province (22373704D). The central government guides local funds for science and technology development (236Z1812G). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for material characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.112391.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
